This is an enhancement adding a `coeffs` property to polynomials, as discussed in https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26401.
The reason is that a lot of people get confused by `P.coef`, because this is not the coefficients they are looking for. This new property will offer them an easy access to `P.convert().coef`, i.e. what they are actually looking for, and explain the difference with `P.coef`.
Best regards,
Olivier

I exposed the question on the mailing list. No clear answer came from it. There are still two possibilities:
(A) Create a new property: add a `P.coef_natural` property, with a suitable documentation ; maybe also add a `P.coef_internal` property. There would be no change to the existing code (only addition of properties). 
(B) Change `P.coef` attribute into a property, with a suitable documentation. Hide `P.coef` attribute into `P._coef` (change existing code). Do not create more properties (unlike A). This does not change the API.

So, what do we do? I feel that people prefer (B) because they are wary of any API change. However the current Pull Request implements (A).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2405916207/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26401","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26401/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26401/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26401/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26401","id":2285591494,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86IO1vG","number":26401,"title":"ENH: Change behavior for `numpy.polynomial.polynomial.Polynomial.fit` coefficients","user":{"login":"Lolcroc","id":4014599,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwMTQ1OTk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4014599?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Lolcroc","html_url":"https://github.com/Lolcroc","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Lolcroc/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Lolcroc/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Lolcroc/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Lolcroc/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Lolcroc/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Lolcroc/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Lolcroc/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Lolcroc/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Lolcroc/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":9,"created_at":"2024-05-08T13:22:12Z","updated_at":"2024-08-16T23:20:14Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Currently polynomial fits work as follows
```python
x = [0, 1]
y = [0, 1]
fit = Polynomial.fit(x, y, deg=1)

print(f"Coefficients for 1-order fit on {x=}, {y=}:")
print(f"Raw: {fit.coef}")
print(f"Converted: {fit.convert().coef}")
```
which prints
```
Coefficients for 1-order fit on x=[0, 1], y=[0, 1]:
Raw: [0.5 0.5]
Converted: [-1.66533454e-16  1.00000000e+00]
```

The result for the raw `fit.coef` is unexpected. The fit is scaled with respect to its current domain, which is by default the endpoints of `x`. The `fit.convert().coef` gives the expected result, but this extra call is somewhat awkward and can easily be overlooked.

### Proposed new feature or change:

I would prefer to set `domain=[-1, 1]` as a default argument of `numpy.polynomial.polynomial.Polynomial.fit`, just as it is for the `numpy.polynomial.polynomial.Polynomial` class. This creates commonality, but also avoids the unexpected outcome as in the above example.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26401/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26401/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2294437831","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26401#issuecomment-2294437831","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26401","id":2294437831,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86IwlfH","user":{"login":"olcc","id":5873395,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4NzMzOTU=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5873395?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/olcc","html_url":"https://github.com/olcc","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/olcc/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/olcc/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/olcc/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/olcc/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/olcc/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/olcc/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/olcc/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/olcc/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/olcc/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-08-16T23:20:13Z","updated_at":"2024-08-16T23:20:13Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"@ggggggggg: Note that the second name you suggest is wrong. ```python
```python
x = [0, 1]
y = [0, 1]
fit = Polynomial.fit(x, y, deg=1)

print(f"Coefficients for 1-order fit on {x=}, {y=}:")
print(f"Raw: {fit.coef}")
print(f"Converted: {fit.convert().coef}")
```
which prints
```
Coefficients for 1-order fit on x=[0, 1], y=[0, 1]:
Raw: [0.5 0.5]
Converted: [-1.66533454e-16  1.00000000e+00]
```

The result for the raw `fit.coef` is unexpected. The fit is scaled with respect to its current domain, which is by default the endpoints of `x`. The `fit.convert().coef` gives the expected result, but this extra call is somewhat awkward and can easily be overlooked.

### Proposed new feature or change:

I would prefer to set `domain=[-1, 1]` as a default argument of `numpy.polynomial.polynomial.Polynomial.fit`, just as it is for the `numpy.polynomial.polynomial.Polynomial` class. This creates commonality, but also avoids the unexpected outcome as in the above example.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26401/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26401/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2294506507","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26401#issuecomment-2294506507","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26401","id":2294506507,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86Iw2QL","user":{"login":"olcc","id":5873395,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU4NzMzOTU=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5873395?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/olcc","html_url":"https://github.com/olcc","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/olcc/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/olcc/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/olcc/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/olcc/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/olcc/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/olcc/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/olcc/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/olcc/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/olcc/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-08-17T00:55:22Z","updated_at":"2024-08-17T00:55:22Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Pull request: https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27232","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2294506507/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Consider the code below (in the "code example" section). The `domain` is changed between `P` and `Q`.
However, I feel that `Q` should keep the same `domain` as `P` when `kind` is prescribed. 
I think this would minimize numerical error for the resulting `Q`.
I think that `ABCPolyBase.convert()` should be changed to include:
```
if kind is None:
  kind = inherit = self.__class__
else:
  inherit = self
if domain is None:
  domain = inherit.domain
if window is None:
  window = kind.window
```

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
from numpy.polynomial import Chebyshev
P = Chebyshev.fit([0, 10, 20, 30, 40], [3, 7, 7, 3, 6], 3)
Q = P.convert(kind=Polynomial)
print(f"Domain for P: {P.domain} and Q: {Q.domain}")
# Domain for P: [ 0. 40.] and Q: [-1.  1.]
```

I think this would minimize numerical error for the resulting `Q`.
I think that `ABCPolyBase.convert()` should be changed to include:
```
if kind is None:
  kind = inherit = self.__class__
else:
  inherit = self
if domain is None:
  domain = inherit.domain
if window is None:
  window = kind.window
```

```python
from numpy.polynomial import Chebyshev

print(f"Domain for P: {P.domain} and Q: {Q.domain}")
# Domain for P: [ 0. 40.] and Q: [-1.  1.]
```

After updating my installation, I observed that the interactive mode is not active anymore for scripts in IPython. This seems to be a regression. The attached script contains description and minimal example.

Script to launch under IPython 8.14.0, with Python 3.11.3, matplotlib 3.7.1 :

$ ipython3.exe --pylab
> %run pb_interactive.py

Result: 
- The interactive mode is not activated inside the script.
- The figure is blocking the IPython interface.

Expected: 
- Interactive mode should be active by default.
- The figure should not block the interface.
- The last line shoud have been have been executed.

Workaround: 
- Run the ion() command from inside the script (uncomment the corresponding line below).
- But this should not be necessary.

```
EXPR.args[0].args[1] = NEW_SUB_EXPRESSION   # Impossible
epath("/[1]/[0]", EXPR, lambda subexpr: NEW_SUB_EXPRESSION)   # Works
```

Could the situation be improved in such a way that the two lines below would work?
```
EPath("/[1]/[0]").set(EXPR, NEW_SUB_EXPRESSION)
epath("/[1]/[0]", EXPR, repl=NEW_SUB_EXPRESSION)
```
